data.title: Wasatch U.S. Select Fund: 6 Stocks That Showed Strong Revenue And Earnings Growth
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Wasatch U.S. Select Fund Q3 2025 Commentary.

BellRing Brands, Inc.

BellRing Brands, Inc. (BRBR) was the largest detractor from Fund performance during the quarter. BellRing is a consumer-staples company known for its Premier Protein line and other convenient-nutrition products. What’s weighed on the stock most is that one of BellRing’s club channel partners is expanding the floor space of the protein shake category and giving some space to other competitors. We spoke with management after that news and reevaluated the business. We came out of that work with continued conviction in the company. We believe BellRing has maintained its leadership position, and that this floor space expansion reflects the health of the category. Looking ahead, we continue to believe BellRing has headroom to double its business, supported by category growth, sourcing share from incumbent brands, and remaining a leader in its space.

Shift4 Payments, Inc.

Another detractor was Shift4 Payments, Inc. (FOUR). A longtime holding in many of our small-cap funds, Shift4 provides payment processing solutions for hospitality, retail, and e‑commerce businesses. Fundamentals for the company remain solid, as organic revenues grew in the high-teens percentages in the most recent quarter. But the stock has been down in part due to concerns about how a softer macroeconomic environment could affect the company. Additionally, investor sentiment on the company became less favorable after its acquisition of Global Blue, which is a foray into a new customer segment for Shift4. The acquisition also means the company is taking on more debt. Acquisitions can sometimes cause investors angst since they take time to integrate into a company’s normal operations. However, we have confidence in Shift4’s leadership and their ability to execute on Global Blue.

Inspire Medical Systems, Inc.

Inspire Medical Systems, Inc. (INSP) also detracted. This is an example of a company for which we reviewed the investment thesis and decided to move on. We sold the position during the quarter. Inspire develops implantable neurostimulators for treating obstructive sleep apnea. The company’s share price declined in August after management downgraded full-year 2025 guidance due to delays in the U.S. commercial rollout of its next- generation InspireV system. The delayed rollout of InspireV raised questions about execution by management. Additionally, concerns are growing about how GLP-1 drugs may affect Inspire’s addressable market.

Medpace Holdings, Inc.

Medpace Holdings, Inc. (MEDP) was the top contributor to Fund performance. The contract research organization provides drug development, clinical trial management, and regulatory services to biotech, pharmaceutical and medical-device companies. Medpace shares jumped after management reported strong revenue and earnings growth and raised full-year guidance. We believe the company’s results are particularly impressive in light of the fact that there’s been a slowdown in the biotech funding cycle.

Fabrinet

Another top contributor was Fabrinet (FN). The company designs, manufactures and tests complex optoelectronic and photonic components, modules, and precision optical assemblies for customers in a variety of industries. Some of Fabrinet’s products are used in data centers, which ties the company somewhat to artificial intelligence (AI). AI has been a popular theme among investors, and likely helped drive the stock higher. However, we don’t hold Fabrinet’s stock as a thematic play on AI. The company is experiencing strong adoption of its products by both new and existing customers. We believe it’s well positioned to grow regardless of AI.

Nova Ltd.

Nova Ltd. (NVMI) also contributed. Nova sells metrology and process control systems used in semiconductor manufacturing. Like Fabrinet, the firm benefits from the increasing use of AI. Nova’s ties to the AI theme likely helped lift the stock during the quarter. But here too, we aren’t investing in Nova as part of a thematic view, and we don’t view it as a speculative business. The company has a strong track record of earnings growth, in our view, and we believe its products will continue to be in high demand as semiconductor manufacturing becomes more complex. (Current and future holdings are subject to risk and change.)

data.title: Wasatch U.S. Select Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Overview

U.S. stocks continued to rally in the third quarter. The Federal Reserve’s interest rate cut helped buoy stocks, as did economic data showing that, while there may be some pockets of concern, the U.S. economy remained steady. In this environment, the Russell Midcap® Growth Index was up 2.78%. The Wasatch U.S. Select Fund—Investor Class did not keep pace and finished the period down -1.93%.

We owned a handful of stocks that fell during the period. Given the concentrated nature of the Fund, those holdings had a large effect on results. A few of the companies reported slower revenue growth and their stocks were down substantially. For most of those companies, investors seemed to be debating whether the slowdown was due to the macroeconomic environment or to competitive pressures.

We have worked through that same debate. One of the ways in which we monitor whether the growth potential and quality of a company is still intact is through our “critical questions.” For every company in which we invest, we create two or three critical questions designed to capture long-term insights about the business that if we knew the answer today would cause us to significantly change our position. These questions help us focus our research on where we think the company could be in five or 10 years.

For Fund holdings that detracted from quarterly performance, we reviewed our critical questions again. Although we sold a few stocks, we believe the long-term investment thesis remains intact for most of the holdings that detracted from performance.

Another reason the Fund underperformed is because, in much of our coverage universe, stocks of low-quality companies have been in favor. This is a direct headwind for our investment process, which seeks to identify high- quality, long-duration growth companies. We can’t predict when the period favoring low-quality companies will end. But periods of outperformance by low-quality stocks have historically been short-lived. The stocks of high-quality businesses have outperformed over the long term. We believe that will continue to be the case.

(Current and future holdings are subject to risk and change.)

Outlook

While we were disappointed with the Fund’s performance this quarter, we aren’t deviating from our investment process. During Wasatch’s 50-year history, we've learned the importance of staying committed to our philosophy and process, as opposed to chasing what’s currently “working” in the market. We’ll always do our best to be open- minded about new opportunities that could unfold, but we’ll stay true to our discipline of finding high-quality, long- duration growth companies.

As we look across the Fund, we remain encouraged by the fundamentals of our companies, and by the high- quality nature of these businesses. Earnings growth for most companies held by the Fund remains strong. This makes us excited about the Fund’s return potential over a three- to five-year time horizon.

Thank you for the opportunity to manage your assets.

data.title: Thrivent Small Cap Growth Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Executive Summary

  • The Fund underperformed the Russell 2000 Growth Index during the third quarter.
  • The Fund underperformed the Russell 2000 Growth Index during the last 12-months.
  • From a sector standpoint, the biggest changes were the following: Consumer Discretionary overweight increased, Financials underweight decreased, Industrials overweight decreased, Health Care underweight increased.
  • These changes are more a product of new idea timing rather than a tactical change.

Performance factors

Thrivent Small Cap Growth Fund underperformed the Russell 2000® Growth Index for the quarter. The underperformance was driven primarily by stock selection in the Health Care, Information Technology, and Industrials sectors.

In Health Care, CorVel Corporation (CRVL), a healthcare managed care and claims management organization, underperformed in the quarter as the company’s largest segment, patient management, experienced softer growth and decreasing margins. Inspire Medical Systems, Inc (INSP), a medical technology company that has developed and commercialized minimally invasive solutions for obstructive sleep apnea, saw growth slow amid a national rollout of their new InspireV product. In addition, margin expectations were lowered as the company intends to spend significantly more on marketing in the coming quarters. In Information Technology, a majority of the Fund’s underperformance came in the application software industry where several names the Fund owned underperformed and in the semiconductor technology industry, where several names the Fund did not own outperformed, causing a cumulative drag on performance. In Industrials, all the underperformance came from one company that the Fund did not own. This company was evaluated and did not meet the investment process at this time.

Partially offsetting these negatives were positive contributions from CECO Environmental Corp, an industrial technology company focused on delivering solutions for management of air quality, water treatment, and emissions, Modine Manufacturing Company (MOD), a thermal management company focused on manufacturing mission-critical solutions for data centers, and Installed Building Products Inc (IBP), a building products company serving the US residential industry.

For the last 12 months the Fund underperformed the Index with most of the underperformance due to poor stock selection in Health Care, Industrials, and Information Technology sectors.

The underperformance in Health Care was driven largely by stock selection. RxSight Inc (RXST), a medical technology company that pioneered the world’s first post-implant adjustable intraocular lens for cataract surgery, saw growth in utilization for procedures stall amid macro concerns and recent launches of competitive products. Inspire Medical Systems and CorVel Corporation contributed negatively, as discussed above. In Industrials, a majority of the underperformance in the sector came from two companies which the Fund did not own. Similarly, in Information Technology, all the sector underperformance came from two companies which the Fund did not own. In both these situations, the names were evaluated and did not meet the Fund’s investment process at this time. Additionally, Onto Innovation (ONTO), a manufacturer of process control solutions and inspection systems, has product leadership in several growth categories that are gaining momentum, but has failed to meet lofty expectations thus far for continued AI (artificial intelligence) demand and expansion.

Offsetting the negative performance in Health Care, Industrials, and Information Technology was positive stock selection in communication services and energy sectors.

While sector positioning of the Fund is a by-product of our stock selection process, relative sector weights are monitored and managed in the context of a rigorous approach to risk management. In the quarter, Health Care underweight was increased and Consumer Staples overweight was reduced as names were sold where the thesis played out or was wrong. The Consumer Discretionary and Information Technology overweight was increased as new names were added to the Fund.

Portfolio outlook

With the current level of business uncertainty and the Federal Funds rate remaining at elevated levels, it is unclear by how much and how long the economy will be impacted. However, investor sentiment about the economy fluctuates much more rapidly and with greater amplitude than actual changes in economic activity. These swings in sentiment often present opportunities. As a result, positions were added to or initiated in consumer discretionary and information technology, while other positions were reduced or sold as investment theses played out or proved no longer valid (health care and consumer staples). Our focus remains on finding quality growth companies with significant opportunities for growth in sales, income, and free cash flow.

data.title: Citigroup: Turnaround Thesis Is As Strong As Ever
data.description:
Back in August, I stated that the turnaround for Citigroup, Inc. (C)(CITI:CA) was starting to gain momentum as both the top and bottom lines saw acceleration. With the P/B ratio below the sector median, I concluded that the risk/reward was attractive and reiterated my buy rating. The stock has since performed decently, as there have been gains of just over 5% since the publishing of that article. The bank reported their earnings just a few weeks back, and today, we will be conducting another analysis.

In the below discussion, further signs of a turnaround are shown. Revenue growth continued to accelerate with strength in all segments, and operating efficiency was able to lead to strong profitability. Guidance was barely changed for the full year, and so the outlook remains positive at this point. The P/B ratio has expanded very slightly since my previous article, but given the increasingly clear signs of a turnaround, I would say that the valuation still offers ample opportunity now. As a result, I am maintaining my buy rating on Citigroup.

Revenue Growth Accelerates

For 2025 Q3, Citigroup reported total revenues of $22.1 billion, and that is up 9% YoY. We will talk a bit more about their segments in just a second, but as you can see above, all of their five main segments saw good YoY growth in the quarter, and so there was widespread strength in their business. The company-wide top line growth rate is another acceleration as 8% of growth was reported in their Q2. Of course this is an encouraging sign and shows that the turnaround continues to progress in terms of business activity. Furthermore, they beat analyst expectations by a rather large margin of $907.32 million, and so Q3 was really a highly respectable quarter for the bank.

Segments Overview

Services

Firstly, CEO Jane Fraser stated that "Services posted its best quarter ever with revenues up 7%" and so it should be clear that the strength of this segment was significant. They reported robustness in Treasury & Trade Solutions and in Securities Services as growth was 7% in both units. A highlight was net interest income as there was an overall 11% increase YoY as they saw higher deposit balances and deposit spreads. With the Federal Reserve on a potential path to more rate cuts, investors should monitor to see if their spreads will be pressured in the coming quarters. Overall operating efficiency was high in this segment as expenses only increased by 5%.

Markets

Despite calmer markets over the summer than back in the spring months after Liberation Day, Citigroup was still able to post robust Markets results. Total revenues were up 15% YoY, and while both Fixed Income and Equity saw good growth, Equity led the way in terms of growth at 24%. In terms of Fixed Income, they saw particularly strong performance in their Rates and Currencies as client activity was high. As for Equity, derivatives saw robust activity as well. Like their Services segment, expenses were up just 5%, and so the bank's efficiency actions seem to be working out quite effectively.

Banking

Their Banking segment saw very encouraging results. Firstly, total revenues increased by 34%, but the breakdown of these revenues is where the good news is. Investment Banking revenues saw a 23% increase, and so it seems that dealmaking is really making a significant comeback for the company and perhaps for the entire sector. The government shutdown may weigh on Q4 investment banking results but overall, Q3 results are a good sign. Another good sign is the 49% revenue increase in Corporate Lending. Despite worries of a global economic slowdown, the strength in this unit shows that economic activity remains more than resilient at this point. Lastly, expense management in this segment was superb, as there was only a 2% increase YoY.

Wealth

For their Wealth segment, revenues were up by 8%, but there was some weakness in Wealth at Work. Nonetheless, net interest income was up by 8% YoY as there were higher deposit spreads as well. Like in their Services segment, it'll be interesting to see how these spreads hold up after 2 rate cuts and perhaps more rate cuts in the near future. Again, operating efficiency was strong as expenses increased by just 4% YoY.

U.S. Personal Banking

Lastly, while there have been significant worries about U.S. consumer confidence, there are signs in this segment that spending is still holding up. For U.S. Personal Banking as a whole, revenues increased by 7%. The largest contributor to revenues was Branded Cards with revenues up 8%, and so it seems that consumers are still spending despite an uncertain tariff environment. Expenses in this segment were flat, which is a good sign, and while there were some U.S. card credit losses, overall credit costs were down 4% YoY, so there shouldn't be too much concern here.

EPS & Returns

With top-line growth having accelerated from Q2 and expense control shown to be strong in each of their five segments, it can be assumed that their EPS and returns are strong. For EPS, they reported $1.86, up 23% YoY. That is actually a bit of a deceleration in growth compared to Q2's 29%, but nonetheless, with bottom line growth greatly exceeding the top-line growth rate, profitability was strong in the quarter. They also managed to beat expectations by a good margin of $0.14. In percentage terms, their bottom line beat even more than their top line, and so analysts were likely impressed by their expense management.

In terms of return ratios, there were significant improvements as well. ROE is up from 6.2% to 7.1% YoY, while ROTCE expanded from 7.0% to 8.0%. These are no small improvements, and so investors should be very happy with the profitability and capital efficiency trends they are seeing. Lastly, it is worth briefly mentioning here that they returned ~$6.1 billion to shareholders in the quarter, and their payout ratio is at 176%. That should be another cause of shareholder delight.

Full Year Outlook

I discussed Citigroup's full-year guidance in my previous article and concluded that the outlook remained positive. After their Q3, there were very minor changes, and so I will only briefly discuss them here. For revenues, they raised their guidance from around $84 billion to now higher than $84 billion, and so essentially they delivered a beat and raise for the top line. Net interest income excluding markets guidance was also raised. However, estimated expenses for the full year was also increased from around $53.4 billion to higher than $53.4 billion and so that might offset some of that revenue strength. Other than that, not much else has changed, and so overall I would say that 2025 continues to look like a solid year for the bank.

Valuation Still Attractive

In my previous article, the P/B ratio was at a reading of 0.896, and so there has been some modest expansion since then to 0.933. I believe this valuation continues to offer a good risk/reward to investors. In my August update, I found strong indications that the turnaround was gaining momentum, and in today's analysis, I found no different. Revenue growth was strong throughout their segments even as the macro environment remained shaky. Furthermore, as a result of the superb expense management, operating efficiency was high, and overall profitability of the business was rewarded. I would say Citigroup's Q3 results provide further evidence that the turnaround is happening, and so the very mild valuation expansion since my previous article is really not a deterrent for long term investors. Furthermore, Seeking Alpha shows that their P/B is still more than 20% below the Financials sector's median, and so I would say the stock is still quite cheaply valued considering the strengthening fundamentals.

Risks

I already mentioned two risks above. Spread pressure from potentially more rate cuts from the Fed as well as an investment banking slowdown in Q4 due to the government shutdown. I believe another potential risk would be if the Fed doesn't cut enough. Sure, more rate cuts may lead to lower spreads and lower profitability for Citi, but on the other hand, if the economy stalls due to the lack of additional rate cuts, the negative effects may be even worse. Recall from above that Corporate Lending was a key growth driver in their Q3. Well, if the economy slows, companies will have less incentive to borrow to fund their original growth strategy. With some Fed officials indicating the preference for holding rates as they are now, this is definitely something investors should have in mind.

Conclusion

Citigroup was on a path to recovery, and that continues to be the case. As stated just above, the fundamentals are improving on many measures, and so it seems that CEO Jane Fraser is doing a great job at improving key areas for the bank. Shareholders should also be cheering the strong ROE and generous capital returns, and so investors looking for income should be tempted. The valuation in the form of the P/B ratio has expanded slightly since August, but the deep discount to the sector shows that Citigroup remains very attractively priced. Therefore, a continued buy rating is justified in my view.

data.title: Capitol Federal Financial: Low Residential Loan Growth To Lift The Bottom Line, Maintain Buy
data.description:
Capitol Federal Financial, Inc. (CFFN) recently reported an EPS of $0.52 for the year ended September 2025, which was slightly higher than my estimate of $0.49 given in the last report I wrote on the company in February 2025. Although earnings have been close to my expectations, the stock price movement has not been as good as I previously hoped. To determine if I need to change my stance, I will start off by looking at Capitol Federal’s balance sheet and earnings potential.

Loan Growth to Slightly Improve

Capitol Federal’s loan portfolio continued to grow at a decent rate in the last quarter of the fiscal year ended September 2025, which was a significant improvement from last year. The loan book grew by 1.1% in 4QFY25, leading to full FY25 growth of 2.6%, as opposed to a decline of 0.8% last year.

Capitol Federal mostly focuses on one-to-four family residential loans. It operates in several states across the nation, with a concentration in the southern states of Kansas, Missouri, and Texas. As shown below, residential loans make up nearly three-quarters of the total loan portfolio.

With interest rates on a gentle downward slope, there is hope that loan growth can be maintained at a low to moderate pace. Lower mortgage rates will boost refinancing of mortgages that were initiated at higher interest rates. Mortgage rates are currently quite low when compared to the last couple of years.

Following the FOMC statement released in October, the Fed Chair made comments that suggested that the Fed will not go for a rate cut in December, as per news reports. However, there are chances of rate cuts next year. The economy is showing signs of a slowdown, which will likely encourage the Fed to cut rates some more. One sign of a slowdown is that manufacturing has declined for an eighth consecutive month in October, according to news reports. Further, monthly job cuts in October surged to a 22-year high, as per news reports.

The Federal Housing Finance Agency president recently confirmed that FHFA is working on introducing a 50-year mortgage, according to news reports. I don’t think the introduction of such a product will help much because young home buyers will be hesitant to lock in such a long-term debt. If somebody is 25 when they contract a 50-year mortgage, then they’ll be 75 years of age by the time the mortgage ends. In case I’m wrong and it ends up being popular, then it could raise home prices. The 50-year mortgage won’t raise the housing supply, so an increase in demand will push up prices.

The Mortgage Bankers’ Association, MBA, is expecting the total mortgage volume to grow by 8% in 2026, especially on the back of refinancing volume, which it expects to grow by 9%. MBA has reduced its estimates since I last wrote a report on Capitol Federal in February 2025, as shown below. (Old forecasts are given in orange, and new forecasts are given in blue).

Considering these factors, I’m expecting the loan portfolio to grow by around 3.0% in the fiscal year ending September 2026, which is better than the growth seen in FY25, but not as good as FY22 or FY23 when interest rates were lower. Further, I’m expecting deposits to grow in line with loans.

Margin Likely to Dip After a Good Year

Capitol Federal’s net interest margin has improved by a decent amount for the last five consecutive quarters. As a result, the average margin in FY25 was 19 basis points higher than the average margin for FY24.

The margin can expand some more because the management anticipates the FRB to continue to reduce rates during FY26, which will reduce interest expenses, as mentioned in the earnings release. Further, the management believes that the loan portfolio yield can remain stable or increase due to an ongoing remixing of the loan portfolio.

On the other hand, there are some threats that can reduce the margin going forward. Capitol Federal focused on attracting deposits through its High Yield Savings Account offering in FY25. These accounts carried very high rates, but their growth didn’t hurt the net interest margin much because they still make a small portion of the total deposit balance. These high-yield deposits rose from $96 million at the end of September 2024, representing 1.6% of the deposit book, to $461 million at the end of September 2025, representing 7.0% of total deposits, as mentioned in the earnings release and shown in the chart below.

Currently, the high-yield savings accounts carry a higher rate of 3.88% compared to Certificates of Deposits (“CD”), which carry an average rate of 3.74%, as per details given in the earnings release. Hence, if Capitol Federal continues to attract deposits through this savings account, then it could eventually materially hurt the net interest margin.

Another factor that could have a negative impact on the margin is interest rate cuts. Due to the concentration in residential mortgages, the average loan yield re-prices at a lag. Meanwhile, the average deposit cost is less sticky as interest-bearing checking, savings, and money market accounts make up 45% of total deposits. The results of the management’s rate simulation model given in the June-ending quarter’s 10-Q filing show that a 100-basis-point rate cut could reduce the net interest income by 1.3% over twelve months. (This estimate isn’t available for the September-ending quarter because the 10-K filing hasn’t been released as of the writing of this report.)

Overall, I’m expecting the net interest margin to dip by just 4 basis points in FY26.

Expecting the EPS to Grow by 9% to $0.57

Capitol Federal reported an EPS of $0.52 for the fiscal year ended September 2025, which was a bit higher than my expectation of $0.49 given in my February report on the company. For FY26, I’m expecting the earnings to continue to grow, mostly on the back of loan growth. However, a slight dip in the margin will likely limit earnings growth.

Meanwhile, I’m expecting the non-interest expenses to grow in the mid-to-high single-digit range. The management mentioned in the earnings release that it was anticipating the salary and employee benefit expense to increase by approximately 9% from fiscal year 2025. This is because of “the expected hiring of additional professionals, merit increases, continued staff compensation alignment with market compensation levels, and the impact of the first full year of compensation for recently hired sales professionals.”

Further, Capitol Federal has pushed out some projects and new initiatives to next year, which will also raise expenses. The management projected the non-interest expense to increase by 7% in FY26 from FY25 in the earnings release.

I’m assuming the non-interest income will be stable at the FY25 level. Further, I’m assuming the provision expense will remain near the level seen in the last quarter.

Overall, I’m estimating an EPS of $0.57 for FY26, up by 9% year-over-year.

The biggest risk to the earnings stems from the loan portfolio’s concentration in residential loans, in my opinion. Due to the reliance on the housing market, headwinds in the housing segment can have a large impact on Capitol Federal’s bottom line.

Maintaining a Buy Rating

I adopted a buy rating on Capitol Federal in my last report that I wrote in February 2025. Although the earnings were close to my expectations, the stock price did not perform as well as I had hoped. The stock price has risen by just 3.75% since my last report.

The total return has been 8.3% since my last report because Capitol Federal offers a high dividend yield of around 5.3%. Capitol Federal has paid a quarterly dividend of $0.085 since 2015, and I think it’s unlikely that the company will break with tradition anytime soon. My earnings estimate and the current dividend suggest a payout ratio of 59.8% for FY26, which is easily sustainable.

To value Capitol Federal, I’m using the historical average price-to-tangible book (“P/TB”) multiple given below.

Multiplying the average P/TB multiple by my TBVPS forecast gives a target price of $8.0 for the end of September 2026.

Along with the historical P/TB multiple, I usually use the historical average price-to-earnings (“P/E”) ratio to value banking stocks, but I don’t think the historical P/E multiple is appropriate for CFFN. This is because the company’s EPS has been too low, and even negative, in the last few years, which diminishes the meaningfulness of the P/E multiple.

Instead of the historical P/E multiple, I’m using the peer average P/E multiple.

CFFN HBNC AMAL MOFG HAFC Average
P/E GAAP ("ttm") 12.25 nm 8.4 13.97 11.21 11.19
P/E GAAP ("fwd") 10.62 nm 8.13 13.85 10.65 10.88
P/TB ("ttm") 0.79 1.65 1.1 1.57 1.04 1.34
P/B ("ttm") 0.79 1.25 1.08 1.34 1.03 1.18

Source: Seeking Alpha

Multiplying the average P/E multiple by my EPS forecast gives a target price of $6.2 for the end of September 2026.

The average price target comes out to $7.1, which suggests a price upside of 11.5% from the November 12 closing market price. Adding the dividend yield of 5.3% to the price upside gives a total expected return of 16.8%. Hence, I think it’s best to maintain a buy rating on Capitol Federal Financial.

I like this company because it has a high dividend yield and a good price upside potential. Further, its earnings are likely to continue to grow mostly on the back of growth in residential loans as market interest rates decline.

data.title: HF Foods: New Numbers, Old Problems
data.description:
Ever since that first-quarter pop, it looks like HF Foods (HFFG) has been running out of magic to impress the market.

That's not by chance. Being one of the top Asian food suppliers in the country, HF Foods is still struggling with two big headaches: weak traffic and tariffs.

Not much has changed in the big picture lately, but if you've been keeping up with my past pieces, you've probably noticed it too. Restaurant traffic started cooling off toward the end of the quarter.

That seems to be my biggest disagreement with the CEO.

There's still the impact from tariffs in terms of inventory, pricing and certainly, foot traffic. Beginning of Q3, we saw it continue to be a little bit softer, but it rebounded nicely towards the end of Q3.

You don’t need to look far to spot this trend. The NRA’s Restaurant Performance Index tells the same story, backing up the numbers I’ve been showing you.

In Q3, the RPI never left negative territory, sliding 0.4% in September and ending the quarter a bit rough.

Looking under the hood, ‘Customer Traffic’ took the hardest hit, dropping 1.3% month over month. And it didn't stop there—every single piece of the Current Situation Index, which basically tracks what's happening right now on the ground, also moved lower.

With ‘Customer Traffic’ down to 98.2, we're pretty much back to where we were in March and April. It’s not the disaster we saw in February, but that steady climb folks were talking about seems to have lost grip.

Now, the effect of this is obvious. With fewer customers at the restaurants, less food will be sold and, consequently, HF Foods will distribute fewer goods.

As I said earlier, FY 2025 and maybe even 2026 look like investment years, with a focus on easing these pressures and running things more efficiently.

Here’s a quick reminder of the investments they’ve got going on:

  1. More private labels are coming out of the gate (the oil bottling operation in Kansas City is one of them) part of the plan to do more on their own and boost margins.
  2. They're also spreading their bets when it comes to sourcing. Shrimp now comes from Peru, Chile, and Ecuador, and they’ve added suppliers in New Zealand and Canada to ease the tariff pain.
  3. An ERP system’s now running company-wide, and they’ve tightened up the sales team, cutting it down and training the rest to work sharper.
  4. And on the logistics side, the Kirkland site in Atlanta nearly doubled its space, while the Great Wall Seafood warehouse in Chicago cut rental costs.

The aim is to narrow the margin gap with larger, well-established peers such as Sysco (SYY), US Foods (USFD) and Performance Food Group (PFGC), all of which have the size to manage customer bankruptcies and cushion tariff impacts.

But as you can see, they've still got a long way to go:

If you followed my advice and went with the big players, you're doing a lot better than anyone who bet on HF Foods.

Turns out what I said aged like a good bottle left in the cellar:

Blue chips are basically the safe haven right now in this industry. They’ve got everything going for them: a large, diversified client base, a massive distribution network, and plenty of tools to deal with inflation, tariffs, or whatever new trade war pops up next Tuesday.

Incremental Gains, Limited Impact

HF Foods managed triple-digit profit growth, although the quarter was still a bit of a mixed story in Wall Street's eyes.

Sales came in a bit stronger than expected at $307 million, a 2.9% increase from a year ago, despite all the talk about slowing orders. EPS came in at $0.08, missing estimates by $0.04, although it still doubled from a year ago.

Even so, the results did not impress the market at all, and the stock fell by a mid-single digit percentage to ~$2.30, the closest it has ever been to its 52-week low.

As the CFO explained, they saw a tailwind from stronger order volumes and a little help from pricing, mostly in the meat, poultry, and seafood categories, which are still pretty inflated.

Well, if we take a look at the numbers coming out of spots like Kura Sushi (KRUS), GEN Korean BBQ (GENK), and Haidilao (HDL), traffic’s still heading south, even as prices keep climbing.

I’d say pricing likely did most of the heavy lifting for HF Foods.

Most margins got a little lift this quarter, helped by SG&A coming in a bit lighter versus revenue and lower interest expenses. The only one that didn't play along was the gross margin, since seafood and other low-margin items took up a bigger slice of the mix.

Just take a look at this:

I can't help but notice HF Foods had to rely on extra debt this quarter to offset the OCF hit from growing operating assets.

This is a problem, and a big one.

HF Foods has $167.5 million in short- and long-term debt (Debt/EBITDA of 4.3x) and, generating unstable cash flow, the company could be at the mercy of payment terms.

All in all, even with the stronger bottom line, it's pretty clear HF Foods is still stuck with the same issues—slow growth, thin margins, and shaky cash flow.

Being the biggest name in the Asian restaurant niche still counts for something, just not as much as it used to.

Still Cheap For A Reason?

Since management hasn't provided any updates on what to expect for FY 2025, I'll stick with my previous base case scenario (consensus revenue of $1.2 billion and EBITDA between $37 and $45 million).

But instead of stacking HF Foods up against the blue chips, let's see how it measures up to other beaten-down names.

For example, we can use United Natural Foods (UNFI), which recently closed its Fort Wayne facility and has similar margins. Others like Mission Produce (AVO) and G. Willi-Food (WILC) also seem like interesting comparisons.

In this case, the group is trading at around 8.9x EBITDA on average.

With the midpoint of this range at approximately $40 million, I'm targeting an EV of $356 million. Excluding net debt ($225.1 million) and dividing by the 53 million shares outstanding, we would arrive at a price target close to $2.40.

This is ~20% below my previous price target of $3!

Now, with the stock hovering around $2.20, there's only a mid-to-high single-digit upside. Not exactly something to get me fired up about HF Foods right now.

That said, I'm sticking with a 'Hold' on this one. I see fair value topping out around $3, since the earnings show they might still have one more solid quarter up their sleeve.

Still, it's a shaky setup with plenty of risks, so I'd rather just watch from the sidelines.

We continue to see opportunities across several long-term themes including U.S. infrastructure spending, a long-term U. S. housing shortage, offshore energy development, and companies that enable the widespread adoption of artificial intelligence (AI).

data.title: Onto Innovation: Accelerating Momentum Into 2026
data.description:
In the summer, I believed that Onto Innovation (ONTO) was putting cash productively back to work, announcing an apparently interesting acquisition of Semilab. Following that deal, the third quarter earnings report was a bit soft, yet momentum seems to reappear in the fourth quarter and into 2026, driving momentum in the shares in recent weeks and months.

While this is to be applauded, that move higher has elevated expectations a bit as well, to levels at which I do not necessarily see real appeal here. This makes me cautious to chase the shares here but keen to keep a close eye on developments from here onwards.

Process Control For Semiconductor Applications

Onto Innovation describes its own business as a capital equipment company, which develops and produces optical process solutions for semiconductor and related markets. Such products and applications focus on improvements in performance, quality, yield, and reliability, thereby helping the entire industry in driving further advancements and acceleration of technological developments.

The company provides such services, similar to industry leaders like KLA Corp. (KLAC) and Applied Materials (AMAT), yet Onto is much smaller, but this gives it a longer runway for growth as well. Over the past decade, the company has quadrupled sales to levels around the billion mark, although that part of this growth resulted from the merger between its predecessors: Rudolph and Nanometrics, causing about 50% dilution in the share base.

Nonetheless, a stock trading in the mere teens in the mid 2010s first hit the $100 mark in 2021, peaked near the $250 mark in 2024, and fell back to the $100 mark over the summer.

Valuation Thoughts

After a temporary boom in 2022, the business has been struggling in recent years, yet growth returned last year. In February of this year, the company reported a 21% increase in fourth quarter sales to $264 million, with revenues trending over a billion per annum again.

The company reported first quarter sales at $267 million, with adjusted earnings reported at $1.51 per share. The company only guided for second quarter earnings at around a quarter of a billion, with some sequential pressure on earnings seen as well.

Trading around the $100 mark over the summer, there were some developments, as the company announced a $545 million deal for Semilab International, adding $130 million in revenues derived from inline wafer contamination monitoring and materials interface characterization.

Valued at 4.2 times sales, this multiple was in line with Onto’s valuation at around the $100 mark, although that these acquired assets have seen 20% compounded annual growth rates since 2021.

With the business posting earnings close to $5 per share, the comment that 10% earnings per share accretion was seen, and net cash balances remain post the deal, all this looked quite compelling.

A Recovery

Since the summer, shares of Onto have gradually risen to the $150 mark, having fallen to $133 per share on the back of some jittery in the wider technology sector in recent weeks, and after the company did not really beat on low third quarter expectations.

Early in July, Onto reported second quarter sales at $253 million and change, up 5% on the year before. Adjusted earnings of $1.25 per share were down seven cents on the year before, with that number looking quite clean, i.e. not adjusted for stock-based compensation expenses. The company issued a downbeat guidance for the third quarter, seeing revenues down to just $210-$225 million, with non-GAAP earnings seen around $0.85 per share.

Third quarter results were just in line with the guidance, as third quarter sales of $218 million were down 13% on the year before, with adjusted earnings of $0.92 per share, being a touch stronger than guided for. Net cash holdings rose to just shy of a billion, all ahead of the Semilab purchase, of course. The third quarter revenue dip is seen ending in the fourth quarter, with sales seen between $250-$265 million, with adjusted earnings seen between $1.18-$1.33 per share, but frankly this is in line with the performance in the first half of the year, despite raging booming markets here in the wider end markets.

With 49 million shares trading at $133, this values equity of the business at $6.5 billion, or $5.5 billion if we factor in net cash (equal to $20 per share ahead of the deal for Semilab) for an earnings multiple in the low-twenties, as growth has been a bit underwhelming this year. However, on the conference call, the company indicated that some 20% growth might be in the works in 2026, growing sequentially throughout the year.

Note that the company sees closing of the Semilab purchase in a couple of weeks, however this contribution is not factored into the guidance for the fourth quarter. With its revenue run rate exceeding $10 million per month, and the purchase contribution about a month to this year's results, there is some upside to the near term results. Moreover, the purchase price has been reduced to $495 million, a fifty million reduction, without much of an explanation given for doing so, something which can be both a positive and negative of course.

And Now?

In between all this, investors should be setting themselves up for solid growth next year, and that is key. With pro forma net cash holdings down to about $10 per share, unleveraged assets trade at $123 per share, while earnings might advance a long way to $6 per share next year, in between organic growth and the Semilab contribution.

I like this set-up, but some momentum has been seen in the share price already, which makes me hesitant to get involved at current levels, but I am keen to keep a close eye on this company and team, hoping for better entry levels over time.

data.title: Siemens Healthineers: Healthcare Remains Attractive After 2025
data.description:
In an inflated market, like the one I believe we currently have, finding value or undervaluation is never the easiest thing. I would go so far as to say it becomes almost impossible after a while, not because some companies are no longer cheap, but because even the cheap companies, in effect, are going to fall when the market does. That means that at a certain point, which of course is yours to determine, it's better to hold money in risk-free methods than to invest it. When that time comes will depend on your individual circumstances, as well as the tax-adjusted risk-free rate you may get for your money where you are.

For me, the rate is so-so. I can get 2-3% risk-free easily, but not much more (which is a lot less than some). It means that my picks, as the market keeps climbing, become fewer and fewer.

It's also the reason I now hold 37% cash in my portfolio, which I have not done since I started investing. There are personal reasons for this as well (some off-market investments), but I would not hold this much cash in any case if I did not see significant risk.

So, it means something when I say that I don't see a significant risk in Siemens Healthineers (OTCPK:SEMHF). This is obviously a company that I've covered and invested in for some time. My position is now just above 1%, and I expect it to grow to 1.5% over time as I add more, slowly.

The company reported its 4Q25 results about a week ago, from the time of submission of this article. I therefore view this as a good opportunity for an update - and to show you why I remain positive on what I consider a very qualitative business.

Let's see what we have here. You can find my last article on the company here.

Siemens Healthineers - Focus on growth, efficiency, and margins

The reason I consider this update quite important is due to the significant share price downturn after the 4Q25/FY25 period. The company fell from close to €50/share native to well below €44/share.

Let's begin by looking at the reason for this - because several buy alerts were triggered on my side, and I immediately bought shares at that time, in line with my overall thesis and recommendation/rating for the business.

At first glance, it's hard to tell what the problem is. The company saw strong equipment growth, and that growth came in from almost every geography. Comparable revenue growth was at almost 6%, which was not only well within the company's guidance but at the upper end of the guidance. The same was true for EPS.

Margin expansion was, in fact, another positive. Imaging, Varian, and AT, all of the important segments, saw significant growth in margin expansion due to scaling, HSD growth, and transformation. All of this was despite challenges in the company's one negative area, China.

If you wanted to argue for what's driving things down, you'd really have to start by arguing things like tariffs (which I believe to be a risk, but something that is developing, rather than permanent). Driving Healthineers down here implies disregarding entirely the company's confirmed underlying margin growth (ex tariffs), as well as the revenue growth.

Imaging as a segment, especially, saw strong trends, with revenue growth above 6% with very strong growth in PETNET and other areas. In other articles, I covered the company's Diagnostics segment. This segment has long been one of the company's core issues, which is also why Healthineers has been targeting a transformation that has been ongoing for some time. Revenue is not yet in the bag in terms of growth, but the margin saw a nice upswing in this quarter.

The company's main peers, and also therefore competition as well as alternative investments, include businesses such as Philips, Abbott (ABT), and Swedish Elekta - although I would consider Elekta to be a niche player here, with little comparable appeal and significant volatility that makes it a hard investment, despite the arguably extremely low valuation for the Swedish peer (more on peers further below in valuation).

Healthineers, on the other hand, is a global giant, owned over 70% by conglomerate Siemens (OTCPK:SIEGY), and has one of the most fundamentally attractive portfolios on the market.

The company is backed by Siemens credit, which is A-rated; it has a market cap of close to €50B, and an estimated forward growth rate of more than 8.5% in AEPS for the coming 3 fiscals, albeit with a dip expected in 2026E, after the 7% AEPS growth in 2025A, as we see here.

That's the interesting thing. Despite growth, despite all the positives, the company still dropped in trading and is only slowly recovering now. If we go through the quarterly results one by one in 2025, we see results exceeding expectations early in the year, a strong 2Q, with raises in 3Q and several upsides. However, tariff impacts were the "talk of the town" during many of the quarters, as well as problems with the Chinese market (stagnation, similar to peer Carl Zeiss (OTCPK:CZMWY). This led to an over 12% share price crash during earnings day, which is what I took advantage of and consider a massive overreaction.

It is my firm opinion, with high conviction, that 2025A was a good year for Healthineers. Tariffs are not something the group can do more about (than it's already doing), and I've already included significant tariff impacts in my PT and FV estimates. I am not including more. The biggest unknowns for Siemens Healthineers, and therefore the biggest risks in 2026E to 2028E, are three.

First, the Chinese market. When, how, and if it will recover. The visibility for this is poor, due above all to increased local competition. This is a real big one.

Secondly, the tariff situation. We'll see how this develops, but as I said, I've included it in my estimates already. I will not be making further adjustments because of it.

Third, and last, the diagnostics transformation. It's ongoing, but again, the visibility for it is poor. It's a multi-year program, and Diagnostics has, for a long time, had issues with profitability. It's my expectation that this will improve, but given inflation, stagnating GDP growth in most of the company's major markets, and other geopolitical factors, time will tell exactly how much the company can turn this around to profit.

However, I consider 2025A a very solid year that confirms the company's premiumization on the market - let's look at what this means for valuation.

Valuation - Upside is increasingly likely at this price, even with market inflation

This company tends to, and still as of today, trade/s at a premium. We have 18.6x P/E normalized for the native ticker SHL, which is what I tend to invest in and look at. The ADR is SEMHF, and I will provide a PT for this one as well.

Your view on Siemens Healthineers really depends on how appealing you think the premiumization is. Because good companies in this peer group and sector tend toward a very high premium - Abbott, for instance, averages 25x P/E - I consider it clear that the market and investors accept a premium for these companies.

However, and obviously so, the lower that premium is, and we can still get market-beating returns, the better your investment safety is.

So, at this time, you can get 15% annualized, which is my hurdle rate - inclusive of the 2.14% dividend from SHL if you buy it at a 21x P/E. How good is that? Well, the company's 10-year average is 24x P/E. The company's 5-year average is 23x. So, an upside at 21x P/E to 15% annualized is a considerable upside with a discount, I would argue, since the upside is 20% annualized at the 5-year average.

I consider the company fairly valued at the 23x P/E on a forward basis. Before, I've implied a fair value at about €71-€73 at most. I maintain that as of this time. This means that my PT of €53/share, which is what I assigned more clearly in my last article, still stands here. I am neither raising nor downgrading it for 2025E, because results were in line not only with company expectations but also with my own expectations as well.

The forecasted decline in 2026E is a minor issue in my view. We may see the company cheaper than now, but even if we do, it doesn't change my long-term thesis. If your conviction is as high as mine is, then this company could potentially be a very solid buy here.

Alternatives may be appealing, but they come with different risk profiles.

Abbott Labs could have upside, but at a higher P/E. The yield is lower as well - so I say "no" here. The upside, even at 25-26x P/E, is less than 14%, so it doesn't clear my hurdle. Carl Zeiss Meditec could be compared, and I do invest in this company as well. However, I would not be doing my job as an analyst with experience if I didn't tell you in no uncertain terms that this company is only a tenth the size of Healthineers and has a much different volatility and short-term risk profile. That is, even though I technically view it as a superior investment in terms of real upside, even to a heavily discounted average.

In closing, I continue to rate Siemens Healthineers as "BUY" and as one of the most attractive (relative to others in risk/upside) investments in this sector. I maintain a PT of €53 and an FV of €72/share, meaning the price at which I would consider rotation of my shares.

Risks and Considerations

The primary risk to this company is one of valuation (still in 2025A). We can still avoid this one if we buy at €46, but less so if we buy at €50, and definitely not (in my view) if we buy at €53/share or above.

However, that's not a problem at the price below €45/share we're seeing now.

It's my view that every indicator and model points to a long-term fair value of between €65 and €73. Above this would be tricky. I don't expect this.

Operational risks for the company are few and far between, but keeping an eye on the diagnostics turnaround and the APAC segment is a good idea for value-conscious investors. I've mentioned this before, and I mention it again here, as other risks are not something the company is significantly in control of (geopoliticals and the like).

At this time, Siemens Healthineers continues to be a Buy. The target for the ADR, SEMHF, comes to $61.34 as of the latest FX.

Thesis

Healthineers is among a class-leading group of companies in the medtech/equipment sector. While not the highest yield available, it nonetheless presents an appealing thesis, with the potential for a double-digit upside at a parent's credit safety of A+/A1. This is worth noting and worth considering, even now in 2025, if you can get the company at the right price, which I think you can in mid-November 2025.

I maintain my previously adjusted share price for the company, with adjustments based on increased volatility as well as growth potential, and point to continued upside in imaging with successes in Varian. Diagnostics remains the biggest challenge for the company, and I don't see this being solved in the near term or easily. My PT represents €53/share, with a fair value upside of 15% annualized to a price of over €65-€73/share-but even in the case of a positive Q3 or Q4, I'm not adjusting it (as I guided for in my last article), owing to some of the company's cyclicality for the short term, and other parts to tariffs and geopolitical trends.

My PT is €53/share for the native, maintained as of this article, and we're a relatively short distance from a downgrade in rating here.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative and well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a price target of €53/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I rate the company "BUY" as of November 2025.

data.title: Columbia Capital Allocation Portfolios Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market overview

The third quarter saw equities reach new highs along with broadened market leadership. The S&P 500 Index and Russell 3000 Index, each a measure of U.S. equities, rose 8.12% and 8.18%, respectively. Within the U.S. equity market, small-cap stocks led the way, as measured by the 12.39% return of the Russell 2000 Index. From a style perspective, growth-oriented stocks outpaced value-oriented stocks within the large-cap segment of the market, but the reverse was true within the mid-cap segment. Within the small-cap universe, growth and value stocks performed similarly. Internationally, the MSCI EAFE Index, measuring developed market equities, returned 4.77%, and the MSCI Emerging Markets Index, measuring emerging markets equities, returned 10.64%, each in U.S. dollar terms, with the latter on pace for its best year since 2009. Fixed-income market returns were modest but positive across the quality spectrum, as concerns around a weakening labor market led the U.S. Federal Reserve to resume cutting interest rates after a nine-month pause. The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index returned 2.03%. High-yield corporate bonds and investment-grade corporate bonds, as measured by the Bloomberg U.S. Corporate High Yield Index and the Bloomberg U.S. Corporate Bond Index, returned 2.54% and 2.60%, respectively. Emerging markets debt returned 3.40%, as measured by the Bloomberg Emerging Markets USD Aggregate Index. Securitized assets, per the Bloomberg U.S. Securitized Index, rose 2.38%. In contrast to the prior quarter, long-duration U.S. Treasuries rallied, with the Bloomberg U.S. Treasury 20+ Year Index returning 2.45%. Short-term U.S. Treasury bills, per the FTSE Three-Month U.S. Treasury Bill Index, rose 1.11%. The global fixed-income market overall, as measured by the Bloomberg Global Aggregate Bond Index, returned 0.60%. Commodities returned 3.65%, per the Bloomberg Commodity Index, with gold enjoying its best year-to-date performance since 1979.

Positioning

Absolute returns in all five portfolios of the Columbia Capital Allocation Series were positive, and, on a relative basis, all five outperformed its new respective blended benchmark, attributable primarily to asset allocation decisions, especially within equity. Style decisions within equity also contributed positively across all five portfolios, more than offsetting the modestly detracting effect of style decisions in fixed income. Underlying fund manager performance overall was modestly additive in all but the most conservative portfolio, where it detracted slightly. Across all five portfolios, overall equity exposure contributed positively. Positioning and underlying fund manager performance within international equities helped most, especially our decision to overweight emerging markets equities. Overweight allocations to U.S. large– and mid-cap equities and U.S. small-cap equities also boosted results. While all segments of fixed income produced positive absolute returns, fixed-income positioning modestly detracted from relative results across all five portfolios. Having an underweight to core fixed income added value, but exposure to high yield, which is now out-of-benchmark, notably detracted from relative results across all five portfolios. Emerging markets bonds exposure also weighed on relative results in all but the most conservative portfolio. A modest use of leverage to fund an overweight in equities helped across all five portfolios. Active currency positioning also added value. During the quarter, we increased the portfolios’ exposure to U.S. large-cap equities. We trimmed the portfolios’ emphasis on growth equities to bring allocations to both growth and value equities in line with respective benchmarks. We reduced the portfolios’ fixed-income exposure overall, given our view that conditions for duration assets, such as U.S. Treasuries, remained unfavorable, but we added exposure to multi-sector high-quality credits through an increased allocation to the Columbia Bond Fund (CBFYX). A modest allocation to commodities was added in the three most aggressive portfolios.

Outlook

At the end of the third quarter, we believed investors were focused on how far the U.S. equity market could keep climbing, bolstered by artificial intelligence companies, and the degree to which the Fed may continue to cut interest rates. At the same time, markets appeared to be shrugging off trade war and tariff uncertainty. We do not anticipate an obvious catalyst to derail the bullish trend. Therefore, we intend to maintain the portfolios’ moderate overweight to equities and their underweight to fixed income, which we view as less compelling. Within equities, we maintain high conviction in our regional preferences, favoring U.S., Canadian and emerging markets equities over Europe and the United Kingdom. Macroeconomic factors support the U.S. and emerging markets positions, and quality factors support Canada and are a driver of an underweight to Europe. Strategically, we believed fixed income will serve as a useful portfolio diversifier, a view supported by lower stock/bond correlation. Within the asset class, we continue to favor higher-quality credit risk, such as investment-grade corporate bonds exposure, over high-yield corporate bonds and emerging market debt.
